feynman.0: An electron is a particle — no question about it: it has mass, it has charge, and it leaves a track you can point at. Now fire that very same electron through a crystal, and it diffracts, the way a wave does. On this page we hand matter a wavelength of its own, λ = h/p, and then we follow the trail of evidence all the way to the strangest experiment of the lot — the double slit run one particle at a time.
feynman.1: By 1924, light had been caught doing both things. It diffracts and interferes like a wave (topic 13), and yet it hands over its energy in discrete photon-sized lumps and carries momentum besides (topic 14). Louis de Broglie looked at that and asked the obvious question: why should the street run only one way? If a wave can carry momentum like a particle, then surely a moving particle ought to have a wavelength of its own — the de Broglie wavelength:
feynman.2: Now since p = mv, the heavier and the faster the thing, the shorter its wavelength. And here's the catch: you only ever see wave behaviour when the wavelength is about the same size as the gap or the obstacle the wave runs into — that's the λ/w ratio of topic 13. That one little condition is the whole reason nobody noticed matter waves for so long.
feynman.3: Take a cricket ball, mass 0.16 kg, bowled at 40 m s⁻¹. Its momentum is p = 0.16 × 40 = 6.4 kg m s⁻¹, so λ = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ ÷ 6.4 = 1.0 × 10⁻³⁴ m. There simply is no gap that narrow anywhere: that wavelength is about 10¹⁹ times smaller than a proton across (a proton being about 1.7 × 10⁻¹⁵ m). The ball diffracts, sure — in principle — but by an amount no instrument you could ever build would notice.
feynman.4: An electron is a different animal. Send one along at 4.0 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹ and its momentum is 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 4.0 × 10⁶ = 3.6 × 10⁻²⁴ kg m s⁻¹, which makes its wavelength 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ ÷ (3.6 × 10⁻²⁴) ≈ 1.8 × 10⁻¹⁰ m — and that's right on the same scale as the spacing between the planes of atoms in a crystal. So a crystal hands you, ready-made, a diffracting structure of just the right size.
feynman.5: Trap You get Planck's constant in two sets of units: 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s and 4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵ eV s. In λ = h/p the momentum is in kg m s⁻¹, which is SI, so you always reach for h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s. The eV s version goes with energies written in electron-volts — never with momenta in kg m s⁻¹. Don't let it sneak in.
feynman.6: Key idea Everything that moves has a de Broglie wavelength, λ = h/p. But you only get to watch the wave behaviour when λ is about as big as the openings on offer — and for everyday things, it just never is.
feynman.7: In 1927, three years after de Broglie spoke up, Davisson and Germer over in the United States and G. P. Thomson up in Scotland aimed beams of electrons at crystals and got diffraction. In Thomson's version you fire the electron beam straight through a thin polycrystalline foil, and out the other side a set of bright rings, one inside the next, shows up on a fluorescent screen. The neatly spaced planes of atoms — a few tenths of a nanometre apart — are doing the diffracting.
feynman.8: Those rings are the giveaway that it's a wave. If electrons were nothing but particles, the beam would just hit the screen in one central splash, fading off toward the edges. Instead you get bright ring, dark ring, bright ring — and they sit at exactly the angles where waves bouncing off the atomic planes line up to reinforce or to cancel.
feynman.9: The clincher is to put it side by side with X-rays, as in Figure 9.1. X-rays are electromagnetic waves with wavelengths that happen to match atomic spacings, and they make ring patterns through the very same foils. Now turn up the electron gun's accelerating voltage until h/p comes out equal to the X-ray wavelength, and watch what happens: electrons and X-rays of the same wavelength produce ring patterns with the same dimensions — at equal wavelength, matter diffracts exactly the way light does. If two patterns come out different sizes, then the wavelengths were different — the rings spread out wider as λ/w grows.
feynman.10: So it's the accelerating voltage that picks the wavelength. Back in Unit 3 we saw that an electron starting from rest and pushed through a potential difference V picks up kinetic energy:
feynman.11: Ishva is running a school diffraction tube. An electron gun accelerates electrons from rest through a potential difference of 500 V and sends them into a thin graphite foil. She treats each electron as a free charge being pushed along through a uniform field. Forget relativity for now.
feynman.12: Formula: here's the chain — Ek = qV, then p = √(2mEk), then λ = h/p.
feynman.13: Substitution: Ek = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 500 = 8.00 × 10⁻¹⁷ J, so p = √(2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 8.00 × 10⁻¹⁷) = 1.207 × 10⁻²³ kg m s⁻¹.
feynman.15: Answer: λ = 5.49 × 10⁻¹¹ m — that's about a quarter of the 2.13 × 10⁻¹⁰ m spacing between atomic planes in graphite, so it sits nicely inside the diffracting range. Now check that assumption we made: v = p/m = 1.3 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹, which is 4.4% of c, so dropping relativity was perfectly fair. And the whole chain runs backwards just as well: measure a wavelength and you've fixed the momentum, the kinetic energy, and so the very voltage that made it.
feynman.16: Connects The electron gun is just Unit 3 hardware: W = qV set the energy scale back there, and now that same accelerating voltage sets a wavelength, by way of p. The electrons haven't changed one bit; it's the wave description that's new.
feynman.17: Now read λ = h/p the other way round and it turns into a statement about light: a photon with wavelength λ carries momentum p. The formula sheet bothers to list this relation twice — once as photon momentum, once as the de Broglie wavelength — because it's really one rule, and it covers light and matter both:
feynman.18: So it follows that a photon and an electron with the same wavelength carry the same momentum. But their energies are a different story. The photon's energy is:
feynman.19: Take λ = 2.0 × 10⁻¹⁰ m. Both the photon and the electron carry p = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ ÷ (2.0 × 10⁻¹⁰) = 3.3 × 10⁻²⁴ kg m s⁻¹ — the same momentum. But the X-ray photon's energy is 6.2 × 10³ eV, while the electron's kinetic energy is Ek = p²/2m = 6.0 × 10⁻¹⁸ J = 38 eV — about 165 times less. Why the difference? It comes down to speed: the photon delivers its momentum travelling at c, so E = pc, whereas the electron carries that same momentum at only 3.6 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹, about an eightieth of c.
feynman.20: Trap Matching wavelengths matches the momenta and the diffraction patterns — never the energies. An electron and an X-ray photon of equal λ make rings you can't tell apart and carry identical p, and yet their energies are off by two orders of magnitude. Slap E = hf onto the electron and you've grabbed the wrong formula: hf is the energy of a photon, and only a photon.
feynman.21: Now, those diffraction patterns are made by beams packed with enormous numbers of particles, and that leaves one nagging objection hanging: maybe the particles in a crowded beam are bumping into one another, and the fringes are really some group effect. The modern double-slit experiment settles it for good. You turn the source down so low that there's only ever one photon — or one electron — inside the apparatus at any moment, and you mark down where each one lands.
feynman.22: Every particle shows up whole, at a single spot: one little dot on the detector, never some faint ghost of the whole pattern. The arrival is particle-like, plain as day. But the dots don't fall evenly. Let the count climb from a few dozen up to many thousands, as in Figure 9.2, and they pile up along the bright fringes of the double-slit pattern, with hardly any landing in the dark ones — at exactly the fringe spacing the wave analysis of topic 13 predicts, once you feed it λ = h/p as the wavelength.
feynman.23: No single particle makes a pattern by itself. With both slits open, each one acts as though it went through both and interfered with itself, and the interference pattern only exists in the statistics, built up over a great many arrivals. Light and matter share this double life — particle-like when they arrive, wave-like in how they spread out — and the single-particle double slit is the cleanest piece of evidence there is for the dual nature of the two of them.
feynman.24: Key idea One particle, one dot; many particles, a wave pattern. Light and matter arrive as particles but spread out as waves — and that is all "dual nature" really means.
feynman.25: Connects This is the last we'll see of the electron in the course: penned into circuits in Unit 1, then accelerated and bent about by fields in Unit 3, and here in Unit 4 it turns out to be neither a clean particle nor a clean wave. And the same story — a trusted model running headfirst into evidence it can't account for — comes round again in topic 17, with relativity.

sagan.0: An electron is, by every measure we can take, a particle — it carries mass, it carries charge, it leaves a track we can see and follow. And yet fire that same electron through the lattice of a crystal, and it spreads and bends like a wave upon water. On this page we grant matter its own wavelength, λ = h/p, and follow the evidence wherever it leads — down at last to a single particle, alone, passing through a double slit.
sagan.1: By 1924, light had revealed both of its faces to us: it diffracts and interferes like a wave (topic 13), and yet it surrenders its energy only in discrete, photon-sized parcels and carries momentum like a hail of tiny bullets (topic 14). Louis de Broglie asked whether the symmetry might run the other way as well. If a wave can carry momentum like a particle, then surely a moving particle ought to carry a wave along with it — the de Broglie wavelength:
sagan.2: Since p = mv, the heavier and the swifter the object, the shorter the wave it trails behind it. And a wave betrays its nature only when its wavelength is comparable to the gap or the obstacle it meets — the λ/w ratio of topic 13. That one condition, simple as it is, explains why the waves of matter hid from us for so very long.
sagan.3: Consider a cricket ball, 0.16 kg of leather and cork, bowled at 40 m s⁻¹. Its momentum is p = 0.16 × 40 = 6.4 kg m s⁻¹, and so its wavelength is λ = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ ÷ 6.4 = 1.0 × 10⁻³⁴ m. No gap so impossibly narrow exists anywhere in the world: this wavelength is some 10¹⁹ times smaller than the very diameter of a proton (about 1.7 × 10⁻¹⁵ m). The ball does diffract, in principle — by an amount no instrument we could ever build will detect.
sagan.4: The electron tells a different story entirely. Moving at 4.0 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹, its momentum is 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 4.0 × 10⁶ = 3.6 × 10⁻²⁴ kg m s⁻¹, and its wavelength is 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ ÷ (3.6 × 10⁻²⁴) ≈ 1.8 × 10⁻¹⁰ m — the very same scale as the spacing between the planes of atoms in a crystal. Here, then, nature has provided a diffracting structure of exactly the right dimension.
sagan.5: Trap Planck’s constant comes to us in two systems of units: 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s and 4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵ eV s. In λ = h/p the momentum is measured in kg m s⁻¹, an SI unit, so you must always reach for h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s. The eV s value belongs with energies written in electron-volts, and never, ever with momenta in kg m s⁻¹.
sagan.6: Key idea Every moving thing in the universe carries a de Broglie wavelength λ = h/p. Its wave nature shows itself only when λ is comparable to the openings it must pass through — which, for the ordinary objects of our daily lives, it never, ever is.
sagan.7: In 1927, only three years after de Broglie had dared to imagine it, Davisson and Germer in the United States and G. P. Thomson in Scotland turned beams of electrons upon crystalline targets — and watched them diffract. In the transmission form of the experiment, a beam of electrons passes clean through a thin polycrystalline foil, and there on a fluorescent screen blooms a set of concentric bright rings. The evenly spaced planes of atoms — a few tenths of a nanometre apart — serve as the diffracting structure.
sagan.8: Those rings are the signature of a wave. Were electrons nothing more than particles, the beam would strike the screen as a single central patch, fading gently toward its edges. Instead we see alternating rings of brightness and darkness, appearing at precisely the angles where waves scattered from the atomic planes fall into step or fall out of it — reinforcing here, cancelling there.
sagan.9: The decisive comparison is with X-rays, as shown in Figure 9.1. X-rays are electromagnetic waves whose wavelengths happen to match the spacings between atoms, and they cast ring patterns through these very same foils. Choose the accelerating voltage of the electron gun so that h/p equals the X-ray wavelength, and electrons and X-rays of the same wavelength produce ring patterns of the same dimensions — at equal wavelength, matter diffracts exactly as light does, with no distinction whatever. And if two patterns differ in scale, then the wavelengths differ — the rings spreading ever wider as λ/w grows.
sagan.10: The accelerating voltage, then, is what sets the wavelength. From Unit 3, an electron accelerated from rest through a potential difference V gains kinetic energy:
sagan.11: Ishva is running a school diffraction tube, in which an electron gun accelerates electrons from rest through a potential difference of 500 V and into a thin foil of graphite. She pictures each electron as a free charge swept along by a uniform field. Ignore relativistic effects.
sagan.12: Formula: the chain runs Ek = qV, then p = √(2mEk), then λ = h/p.
sagan.13: Substitution: Ek = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 500 = 8.00 × 10⁻¹⁷ J, so p = √(2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 8.00 × 10⁻¹⁷) = 1.207 × 10⁻²³ kg m s⁻¹.
sagan.15: Answer: λ = 5.49 × 10⁻¹¹ m — about a quarter of the 2.13 × 10⁻¹⁰ m spacing between the atomic planes of graphite, comfortably within the diffracting range. And we should check the assumption we made: v = p/m = 1.3 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹, which is 4.4% of c, so to set the relativistic effects aside was indeed justified. The chain runs equally well backward: a measured wavelength fixes the momentum, and the momentum fixes the kinetic energy, and that in turn fixes the very voltage that gave the electron its flight.
sagan.16: Connects The electron gun is Unit 3 hardware come back to us: W = qV set the energy scale there, and now that same accelerating voltage sets a wavelength, by way of p. The electrons are the very same; only the wave description is new.
sagan.17: Read λ = h/p in the other direction, and it becomes a statement about light: a photon of wavelength λ carries momentum p. The formula sheet lists this relation twice over — once as photon momentum, once as the de Broglie wavelength — because in truth it is one single rule, embracing both light and matter alike:
sagan.18: From this it follows that a photon and an electron sharing the same wavelength must carry the very same momentum. Yet their energies are not the same at all. A photon’s energy is:
sagan.19: At λ = 2.0 × 10⁻¹⁰ m, both the photon and the electron carry p = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ ÷ (2.0 × 10⁻¹⁰) = 3.3 × 10⁻²⁴ kg m s⁻¹. The X-ray photon’s energy is 6.2 × 10³ eV, but the electron’s kinetic energy is Ek = p²/2m = 6.0 × 10⁻¹⁸ J = 38 eV — fully about 165 times less. The reason is speed itself: the photon delivers its momentum at c, so E = pc, while the electron carries that identical momentum at a mere 3.6 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹, about an eightieth of c.
sagan.20: Trap To match wavelengths is to match momenta and diffraction patterns — but never energies. An electron and an X-ray photon of equal λ trace out identical rings and carry identical p, and yet their energies stand apart by two orders of magnitude. To apply E = hf to the electron is to reach for the wrong formula altogether: hf is the energy of a photon, and of a photon alone.
sagan.21: These diffraction patterns are made by beams holding vast multitudes of particles, and that leaves one stubborn objection still standing: perhaps the particles crowded into a dense beam interact with one another, so that the fringes are merely a collective effect of the throng. The modern double-slit experiment lays the objection to rest. The source is turned down low, lower, until only one photon — or one electron — wanders through the apparatus at any moment, and the arrival point of each is faithfully recorded.
sagan.22: Every particle arrives whole, at a single point: one small, localised dot upon the detector, never a faint ghost of the whole pattern. The arrival is utterly particle-like. And yet the dots do not fall evenly. As the count climbs from a few dozen to many thousands, as shown in Figure 9.2, they gather along the bright fringes of the double-slit pattern, and scarcely any settle in the dark — at exactly the fringe spacing that the wave analysis of topic 13 foretells, once λ = h/p is taken as the wavelength.
sagan.23: No single particle ever makes a pattern. With both slits standing open, each particle behaves as though it passes through both at once and interferes with its own self, and the interference pattern lives only in the statistics, emerging across many thousands of arrivals. Light and matter share this twofold character — particle-like in their arriving, wave-like in their gathering — and the single-particle double slit is the cleanest evidence we possess for the dual nature of them both.
sagan.24: Key idea One particle, one dot; many particles, a wave. Light and matter arrive among us as particles, yet they distribute themselves as waves — and this, in the end, is all that “dual nature” has ever meant.
sagan.25: Connects Here we take our final look at the electron in this course: confined to circuits back in Unit 1, then accelerated and bent by fields in Unit 3, it is described at last, in Unit 4, as neither a pure particle nor a pure wave. And the same old story — a trusted model meeting evidence it simply cannot account for — returns to us once more in topic 17, as relativity.

cox.0: An electron is, by every test we can throw at it, a particle — it has mass, it has charge, it leaves a track you can measure. And yet fire that very same electron through a crystal and it diffracts, spreads, behaves for all the world like a wave. What I find wonderful is that this page gives matter its own wavelength, λ = h/p, and then simply follows the evidence, all the way down to a double-slit experiment run one particle at a time.
cox.1: By 1924 light had been caught behaving both ways at once: it diffracts and interferes like a wave (topic 13), and yet it hands over its energy in discrete photon-sized parcels and carries momentum besides (topic 14). And here is the lovely thought Louis de Broglie had — that the symmetry ought to run both ways. If a wave can carry momentum like a particle, then surely a moving particle should have a wavelength of its own — the de Broglie wavelength:
cox.2: Since p = mv, the heavier and the faster a thing moves, the shorter its wavelength becomes. And wave behaviour only shows itself when that wavelength is something like the size of the gap or the obstacle the wave runs into — the λ/w ratio of topic 13. That single quiet condition is the whole reason matter waves stayed hidden from us for so long.
cox.3: Take a cricket ball, 0.16 kg, bowled at 40 m s⁻¹. Its momentum is p = 0.16 × 40 = 6.4 kg m s⁻¹, and so λ = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ ÷ 6.4 = 1.0 × 10⁻³⁴ m. There is no gap in the universe this narrow: the wavelength is something like 10¹⁹ times smaller than the diameter of a single proton (about 1.7 × 10⁻¹⁵ m). The ball does diffract, in principle — but by an amount no instrument we could ever build would notice.
cox.4: An electron is a different story altogether. At 4.0 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹ its momentum is 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 4.0 × 10⁶ = 3.6 × 10⁻²⁴ kg m s⁻¹, and its wavelength comes out as 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ ÷ (3.6 × 10⁻²⁴) ≈ 1.8 × 10⁻¹⁰ m — and here is the beautiful coincidence, that is just the scale of the spacing between the planes of atoms in a crystal. A crystal, then, hands us a diffracting structure of exactly the right size.
cox.5: Trap Planck’s constant comes to us in two sets of units: 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s and 4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵ eV s. In λ = h/p the momentum is in kg m s⁻¹, which is an SI unit, so always reach for h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s. The eV s value belongs with energies written in electron-volts, and never with momenta in kg m s⁻¹.
cox.6: Key idea Every moving object, every single one, carries a de Broglie wavelength λ = h/p. Wave behaviour only ever shows itself when λ is something like the size of the openings on offer — which, for the everyday things around us, it simply never is.
cox.7: In 1927, just three years after de Broglie had the thought, Davisson and Germer in the United States and G. P. Thomson up in Scotland aimed beams of electrons at crystalline targets — and watched them diffract. In the transmission version of the experiment an electron beam passes clean through a thin polycrystalline foil, and a set of concentric bright rings blooms onto a fluorescent screen. The neatly spaced planes of atoms — a few tenths of a nanometre apart — are doing the diffracting.
cox.8: Those rings are the wave showing its hand. If electrons were particles and nothing more, the beam would land as a single central patch, fading gently towards the edges. Instead you get alternating bright and dark rings, appearing at precisely the angles where waves scattered off the atomic planes fall into step and reinforce, or fall out of step and cancel.
cox.9: The clinching comparison is with X-rays, as shown in Figure 9.1. X-rays are electromagnetic waves with wavelengths that happen to match atomic spacings, and they make ring patterns through the very same foils. Tune the electron gun’s accelerating voltage so that h/p comes out equal to the X-ray wavelength, and electrons and X-rays of the same wavelength produce ring patterns with the same dimensions — at equal wavelength, matter diffracts exactly as light does. And if two patterns come out at different scales, then the wavelengths differ — the rings open out wider as λ/w grows.
cox.10: So it is the accelerating voltage that sets the wavelength. From Unit 3, an electron accelerated from rest through a potential difference V gains kinetic energy:
cox.11: Ishva is running a school diffraction tube, in which an electron gun accelerates electrons from rest through a potential difference of 500 V and into a thin graphite foil. She treats each electron as a free charge accelerated through a uniform field. Ignore relativistic effects.
cox.12: Formula: the chain runs Ek = qV, then p = √(2mEk), then λ = h/p.
cox.13: Substitution: Ek = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 500 = 8.00 × 10⁻¹⁷ J, and so p = √(2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 8.00 × 10⁻¹⁷) = 1.207 × 10⁻²³ kg m s⁻¹.
cox.15: Answer: λ = 5.49 × 10⁻¹¹ m — about a quarter of the 2.13 × 10⁻¹⁰ m atomic-plane spacing in graphite, comfortably inside the diffracting range. And it is worth checking the assumption: v = p/m = 1.3 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹, which is 4.4% of c, so quietly setting relativity aside is justified. The whole chain runs backwards just as happily: a measured wavelength fixes the momentum, which fixes the kinetic energy, which fixes the accelerating voltage that made it in the first place.
cox.16: Connects The electron gun is Unit 3 hardware: W = qV set the energy scale there, and now that same accelerating voltage sets a wavelength, through p. The electrons have not changed at all; only the wave description is new.
cox.17: Read λ = h/p the other way round and it quietly becomes a statement about light: a photon of wavelength λ carries momentum p. The formula sheet lists the relation twice — once as photon momentum, once as the de Broglie wavelength — and that is because it is really one rule, covering both light and matter at once:
cox.18: From which it follows that a photon and an electron sharing the same wavelength carry the very same momentum. Their energies, though, are quite different. A photon’s energy is:

And the reason is simply speed: the photon delivers its momentum at c, so E = pc, while the electron carries that same momentum along at 3.6 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹, about an eightieth of c.
cox.20: Trap Matching the wavelengths matches the momenta, and matches the diffraction patterns — but never the energies. The modern double-slit experiment lays that objection to rest. You turn the source right down, until only one photon — or one electron — is in the apparatus at any moment, and you record where each one lands.

As the count climbs from dozens to many thousands, as shown in Figure 9.2, they gather along the bright fringes of the double-slit pattern, and almost none land in the dark ones — and at exactly the fringe spacing the wave analysis of topic 13 predicts, once you feed in λ = h/p as the wavelength.
cox.23: No single particle makes a pattern by itself. With both slits open, each one behaves as though it passes through both at once and interferes with itself, and the interference pattern only exists statistically, built up across many, many arrivals. Light and matter both share this double character — particle-like in how they arrive, wave-like in how they spread — and the single-particle double slit is the cleanest evidence we have for the dual nature of the two.
cox.24: Key idea One particle, one dot; many particles, a wave pattern. Light and matter arrive as particles but distribute themselves as waves — and that, in the end, is all that “dual nature” really means.
cox.25: Connects This is the last we see of the electron in the course: confined to circuits back in Unit 1, then accelerated and deflected by fields in Unit 3, and now in Unit 4 described as neither a pure particle nor a pure wave. And the same pattern — a settled model running into evidence it simply cannot explain — comes round again in topic 17, as relativity.

So Louis de Broglie asked a beautiful question — what if the symmetry runs both ways? If a wave can carry momentum like a particle, then a moving particle ought to come with a wavelength of its own — the de Broglie wavelength:
nye.2: Now since p = mv, the heavier and faster the object, the tinier its wavelength gets. And here’s the thing — wave behaviour only shows up when the wavelength is about the same size as the gap or obstacle the wave runs into — that’s the λ/w ratio of topic 13. That one single condition is exactly why nobody spotted matter waves for the longest time!
nye.3: Check this out with a cricket ball — mass 0.16 kg, bowled at 40 m s⁻¹. Its momentum is p = 0.16 × 40 = 6.4 kg m s⁻¹, so λ = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ ÷ 6.4 = 1.0 × 10⁻³⁴ m. There’s no gap anywhere this narrow — that wavelength is about 10¹⁹ times smaller than the diameter of a proton (around 1.7 × 10⁻¹⁵ m)! In principle the ball does diffract — but by an amount no instrument on Earth could ever pick up.
nye.4: An electron, though — totally different story! Send it at 4.0 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹ and its momentum is 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 4.0 × 10⁶ = 3.6 × 10⁻²⁴ kg m s⁻¹, which makes its wavelength 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ ÷ (3.6 × 10⁻²⁴) ≈ 1.8 × 10⁻¹⁰ m — the very same scale as the spacing between the atomic planes in a crystal! So a crystal hands us a diffracting structure right at the size we need.
nye.5: Trap Watch out — Planck’s constant comes in two unit systems: 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s and 4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵ eV s. In λ = h/p the momentum is in kg m s⁻¹, an SI unit, so you always reach for h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s. That eV s value belongs with energies in electron-volts — never with momenta in kg m s⁻¹!
nye.6: Key idea Every single moving object has a de Broglie wavelength λ = h/p. But the wave behaviour only becomes visible when λ is about the size of the openings on offer — and for everyday objects, that never, ever happens!
nye.7: In 1927, just three years after de Broglie threw down his idea, Davisson and Germer over in the United States and G. P. Thomson up in Scotland fired beams of electrons straight at crystalline targets — and there it was: diffraction! In the transmission version of the experiment, an electron beam shoots through a thin polycrystalline foil and a set of concentric bright rings lights up on a fluorescent screen. The regularly spaced planes of atoms — just a few tenths of a nanometre apart — do the diffracting.
nye.8: And those rings? That’s wave evidence, plain and simple! If electrons were nothing but particles, the beam would smack the screen in one central patch, fading out toward the edges. But that’s not what you get — instead, alternating bright and dark rings show up at exactly the angles where waves scattered off the atomic planes reinforce and cancel each other.
nye.9: Now here’s the clincher — compare it with X-rays, like you see in Figure 9.1. X-rays are electromagnetic waves with wavelengths that match atomic spacings, and they make ring patterns through the very same foils. Dial the electron gun’s accelerating voltage so that h/p equals the X-ray wavelength, and watch — electrons and X-rays of the same wavelength produce ring patterns with the same dimensions — at equal wavelength, matter diffracts exactly the way light does! And if two patterns differ in scale, then the wavelengths differ — the rings spread out further as λ/w grows.
nye.10: So the accelerating voltage is what sets the wavelength. Remember from Unit 3 — an electron accelerated from rest through a potential difference V picks up kinetic energy:
nye.11: Ishva is running a school diffraction tube where an electron gun accelerates electrons from rest through a potential difference of 500 V into a thin graphite foil. She treats each electron as a free charge accelerated through a uniform field. Ignore relativistic effects.
nye.12: Formula: here’s the chain — Ek = qV, then p = √(2mEk), then λ = h/p.
nye.13: Substitution: Ek = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 500 = 8.00 × 10⁻¹⁷ J, so p = √(2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 8.00 × 10⁻¹⁷) = 1.207 × 10⁻²³ kg m s⁻¹.

nye.16: Connects That electron gun? It’s straight-up Unit 3 hardware: W = qV set the energy scale there, and now that same accelerating voltage sets a wavelength through p. The electrons haven’t changed one bit — it’s the wave description that’s brand new.
nye.17: Now read λ = h/p the other way round and watch it turn into a statement about light: a photon of wavelength λ carries momentum p. nye.18: So here’s what follows: a photon and an electron with the same wavelength carry the same momentum. But their energies? Totally different! A photon’s energy is:
nye.19: Take λ = 2.0 × 10⁻¹⁰ m — both a photon and an electron carry p = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ ÷ (2.0 × 10⁻¹⁰) = 3.3 × 10⁻²⁴ kg m s⁻¹. nye.21: Now here’s a fair objection. Diffraction patterns are made by beams packed with huge numbers of particles, which leaves one loose end: maybe the particles in a dense beam are bumping into each other, so the fringes are just some collective effect. nye.23: No single particle makes a pattern on its own. With both slits open, each particle behaves as if it sails through both and interferes with itself, and the interference pattern only exists statistically, built up across many arrivals. nye.25: Connects And this is the last we’ll see of the electron in the whole course: penned up in circuits back in Unit 1, then accelerated and deflected by fields in Unit 3, and now in Unit 4 it turns out to be neither a pure particle nor a pure wave. That same pattern — a trusted model running smack into evidence it just can’t explain — shows up all over again in topic 17 as relativity!

czerski.0: An electron is about as solid a particle as you could ask for — it has mass, it carries charge, and it leaves a track you can actually point to. And yet fire that same electron through a crystal and it spreads out and diffracts, for all the world like a wave. This page hands matter a wavelength of its own, λ = h/p, and follows the trail of evidence all the way to the strangest result of all: the single-particle double-slit experiment.
czerski.1: By 1924 light had been caught doing both things at once. It diffracts and interferes like a wave (topic 13), yet it hands over its energy in discrete photon-sized lumps and shoves things with momentum (topic 14). Louis de Broglie had the nerve to ask whether the symmetry runs both ways. If a wave can carry momentum like a particle, then surely a moving particle ought to come with a wavelength of its own — the de Broglie wavelength:
czerski.2: Since p = mv, the heavier and faster the thing, the shorter its wavelength gets. And wave behaviour only shows itself when the wavelength is roughly the size of the gap or the obstacle the wave runs into — the λ/w ratio of topic 13. That one simple condition is the whole reason nobody spotted matter waves for so long.
czerski.3: Take a cricket ball, mass 0.16 kg, bowled at 40 m s⁻¹. Its momentum is p = 0.16 × 40 = 6.4 kg m s⁻¹, so λ = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ ÷ 6.4 = 1.0 × 10⁻³⁴ m. There simply is no gap that narrow anywhere in the universe: that wavelength is about 10¹⁹ times smaller than a proton (which is itself only about 1.7 × 10⁻¹⁵ m across). The ball does diffract, strictly speaking — but by an amount no instrument that could ever be built would notice.
czerski.4: An electron is another story entirely. Send it along at 4.0 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹ and its momentum is 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 4.0 × 10⁶ = 3.6 × 10⁻²⁴ kg m s⁻¹, which gives a wavelength of 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ ÷ (3.6 × 10⁻²⁴) ≈ 1.8 × 10⁻¹⁰ m — right on the same scale as the spacing between the planes of atoms in a crystal. So a crystal hands you exactly the right-sized grating to diffract it.
czerski.5: Trap Planck’s constant comes in two flavours: 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s and 4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵ eV s. In λ = h/p the momentum is in kg m s⁻¹, which is pure SI, so you always reach for h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s. The eV s version goes with energies measured in electron-volts, and never, ever with momenta in kg m s⁻¹.
czerski.6: Key idea Every moving thing has a de Broglie wavelength λ = h/p. Its wave behaviour only becomes visible when λ is about the size of the openings on offer — which, for everyday objects, it simply never is.
czerski.7: In 1927, just three years after de Broglie floated the idea, Davisson and Germer in the United States and G. P. Thomson in Scotland aimed beams of electrons at crystalline targets — and watched them diffract. In the transmission version, an electron beam is fired straight through a thin polycrystalline foil, and a set of concentric bright rings blooms onto a fluorescent screen. The neatly spaced planes of atoms — a few tenths of a nanometre apart — are doing the diffracting.
czerski.8: Those rings are the wave caught in the act. If electrons were nothing but particles, the beam would simply splat onto the screen in one central blob, fading out toward the edges. Instead you get alternating bright and dark rings, landing at precisely the angles where waves scattered off the atomic planes line up to reinforce and to cancel.
czerski.9: The clinching comparison is with X-rays, as shown in Figure 9.1. X-rays are electromagnetic waves whose wavelengths happen to match atomic spacings, and they make ring patterns through the very same foils. Tune the electron gun’s accelerating voltage so that h/p comes out equal to the X-ray wavelength, and electrons and X-rays of the same wavelength produce ring patterns with the same dimensions — at equal wavelength, matter diffracts in exactly the way light does. If two patterns come out at different scales, then the wavelengths differ — the rings open out wider as λ/w grows.
czerski.10: So it’s the accelerating voltage that sets the wavelength. From Unit 3, an electron accelerated from rest through a potential difference V picks up kinetic energy:
czerski.11: Ishva is running a school diffraction tube, in which an electron gun accelerates electrons from rest through a potential difference of 500 V and fires them into a thin graphite foil. She treats each electron as a free charge accelerated through a uniform field. Ignore relativistic effects.
czerski.12: Formula: the chain runs Ek = qV, then p = √(2mEk), then λ = h/p.
czerski.13: Substitution: Ek = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 500 = 8.00 × 10⁻¹⁷ J, so p = √(2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 8.00 × 10⁻¹⁷) = 1.207 × 10⁻²³ kg m s⁻¹.
czerski.15: Answer: λ = 5.49 × 10⁻¹¹ m — about a quarter of the 2.13 × 10⁻¹⁰ m atomic-plane spacing in graphite, comfortably inside the range where diffraction shows up. Now check the assumption you made: v = p/m = 1.3 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹, which is 4.4% of c, so brushing relativity aside was perfectly fair. And the chain runs backwards just as well: measure the wavelength and you’ve pinned down the momentum, the kinetic energy, and so the accelerating voltage that made it.
czerski.16: Connects That electron gun is straight out of the Unit 3 toolkit: W = qV set the energy scale there, and now the same accelerating voltage sets a wavelength, by way of p. The electrons haven’t changed at all; only the wave description is new.
czerski.17: Read λ = h/p the other way round and it turns into a statement about light: a photon of wavelength λ carries momentum p. The formula sheet lists the relation twice — once as photon momentum, once as the de Broglie wavelength — because it’s really one rule doing both jobs, covering light and matter at a stroke:
czerski.18: Which means a photon and an electron with the same wavelength carry exactly the same momentum. Their energies, though, are nothing alike. A photon’s energy is:
czerski.19: At λ = 2.0 × 10⁻¹⁰ m, both a photon and an electron carry p = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ ÷ (2.0 × 10⁻¹⁰) = 3.3 × 10⁻²⁴ kg m s⁻¹. The X-ray photon’s energy comes to 6.2 × 10³ eV, but the electron’s kinetic energy is Ek = p²/2m = 6.0 × 10⁻¹⁸ J = 38 eV — about 165 times less. The difference is all down to speed: the photon delivers its momentum at c, so E = pc, while the electron carries that very same momentum at a leisurely 3.6 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹, about an eightieth of c.
czerski.20: Trap Matching wavelengths matches momenta and diffraction patterns — never energies. An electron and an X-ray photon of equal λ make identical rings and carry identical p, yet their energies differ by two orders of magnitude. Reaching for E = hf to find the electron’s energy is a wrong-formula blunder: hf is the energy of a photon, and nothing else.
czerski.21: Diffraction patterns are built from beams holding enormous numbers of particles, which leaves one nagging objection hanging in the air: maybe the particles in a crowded beam jostle one another, and the fringes are just some collective pile-up. The modern double-slit experiment lays that objection to rest. You turn the source down so low that only one photon — or one electron — is in the apparatus at any moment, and you log exactly where each one lands.

But the dots don’t fall evenly. As the tally climbs from dozens to many thousands, as shown in Figure 9.2, they crowd along the bright fringes of the double-slit pattern and steer clear of the dark ones — landing with exactly the fringe spacing the wave analysis of topic 13 predicts, once you feed in λ = h/p as the wavelength.
czerski.23: No single particle makes a pattern on its own. With both slits open, each one behaves as if it goes through both and interferes with itself, and the interference pattern only exists statistically, spread across thousands of arrivals. Light and matter both wear this double character — particle-like when they arrive, wave-like in how they spread — and the single-particle double slit is the cleanest evidence we have for the dual nature of both.
czerski.24: Key idea One particle, one dot; many particles, a wave pattern. Light and matter arrive as particles but spread out as waves — and that, in a nutshell, is what “dual nature” means.
czerski.25: Connects This is the last we’ll see of the electron in the course: penned into circuits back in Unit 1, then accelerated and steered by fields in Unit 3, and now in Unit 4 it turns out to be neither a clean particle nor a clean wave. The same story — a trusted model running headlong into evidence it can’t account for — comes round again in topic 17, with relativity.

In λ = h/p the momentum is reckoned in kg m s⁻¹, an SI measure, so ever and always wield h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s. The eV s value keeps faith with energies told in electron-volts, and never with momenta told in kg m s⁻¹.
greekepic.6: Key idea Every moving body bears a de Broglie wavelength λ = h/p. The wave's nature comes into sight only when λ stands comparable to the openings afforded it — which, for the things of common day, it never does.
greekepic.7: In 1927, three years after de Broglie spoke his prophecy, Davisson and Germer in the land of America, and G. P. Thomson in Scotland, drove beams of electrons against crystalline targets and beheld diffraction. In the transmission rite of the ordeal, an electron beam passes through a thin polycrystalline foil, and a host of concentric bright rings blooms upon a fluorescent screen. The orderly ranks of atomic planes — a few tenths of a nanometre asunder — stand as the diffracting frame.
greekepic.8: The rings are the witness of the wave. Were electrons mere particles and nothing more, the beam would smite the screen in a single central patch, dwindling toward its rim. Instead, bright rings and dark rings rise in turn, at the very angles where the waves scattered by the atomic planes do strengthen one another and undo one another.
greekepic.9: The deciding measure lies in the comparison with X-rays, as is shown in Figure 9.1. X-rays are waves of the electromagnetic kind, their wavelengths matched to atomic spacings, and through these same foils they too cast ring patterns. When the accelerating voltage of the electron gun is so chosen that h/p equals the X-ray wavelength, electrons and X-rays of the same wavelength bring forth ring patterns of the same dimensions — at equal wavelength, matter bends exactly as light bends. Should two patterns differ in their scale, then differ too the wavelengths — the rings spread ever wider as λ/w climbs.
greekepic.10: The accelerating voltage, therefore, ordains the wavelength. From Unit 3, an electron accelerated from rest through a potential difference V gains kinetic energy:
greekepic.11: Ishva tends a school diffraction tube, wherein an electron gun drives electrons from rest through a potential difference of 500 V into a thin graphite foil. Each electron she takes as a free charge borne onward through a uniform field. Let relativistic effects be set aside.
greekepic.12: Formula: the chain runs thus — Ek = qV, then p = √(2mEk), then λ = h/p.
greekepic.13: Substitution: Ek = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 500 = 8.00 × 10⁻¹⁷ J, and so p = √(2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 8.00 × 10⁻¹⁷) = 1.207 × 10⁻²³ kg m s⁻¹.
greekepic.15: Answer: λ = 5.49 × 10⁻¹¹ m — near a quarter of the 2.13 × 10⁻¹⁰ m atomic-plane spacing in graphite, and thus within the diffracting range. Now prove the assumption true: v = p/m = 1.3 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹, which is 4.4% of c, so to set relativistic effects aside is rightly done. And the chain runs backward as readily as forward: a measured wavelength fixes the momentum, the momentum the kinetic energy, and thence the accelerating voltage that begot it.
greekepic.16: Connects The electron gun is the forge-work of Unit 3: W = qV laid down the scale of energy there, and that same accelerating voltage now lays down a wavelength by way of p. The electrons are unchanged; only the wave-telling of them is new.
greekepic.17: Read λ = h/p the other way about, and it becomes a saying concerning light: a photon of wavelength λ bears momentum p. Twice the formula sheet sets down this relation — once as photon momentum, once as the de Broglie wavelength — for it is one decree that holds sway over light and matter both:
greekepic.18: It follows, then, that a photon and an electron of the same wavelength bear the same momentum. Yet their energies are not as one. A photon's energy is:
greekepic.19: At λ = 2.0 × 10⁻¹⁰ m, both photon and electron bear p = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ ÷ (2.0 × 10⁻¹⁰) = 3.3 × 10⁻²⁴ kg m s⁻¹. The X-ray photon's energy is 6.2 × 10³ eV, but the electron's kinetic energy is Ek = p²/2m = 6.0 × 10⁻¹⁸ J = 38 eV — some 165 times less. The cause is speed: the photon delivers its momentum at c, whence E = pc, while the electron bears that same momentum at 3.6 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹, near an eightieth part of c.
greekepic.20: Trap Mark well: to match wavelengths is to match momenta and diffraction patterns — never energies. An electron and an X-ray photon of equal λ cast identical rings and bear identical p, yet their energies stand asunder by two orders of magnitude. To lay E = hf upon the electron is to wield the wrong decree: hf is the energy of the photon, and the photon alone.
greekepic.21: Diffraction patterns are wrought by beams thronged with vast multitudes of particles, and this leaves one objection yet unconquered: the particles in a crowded beam might commune one with another, so that the fringes are but a thing of the throng. The modern double-slit ordeal lays this objection low. The source is dimmed until but one photon — or one electron — abides within the apparatus at a time, and the place where each arrives is set down.
greekepic.22: Each particle arrives entire, at a single point: one bright dot upon the detector, and never a faint likeness of the whole pattern. The arrival is particle-like. Yet the dots fall not evenly. As the tally swells from dozens to many thousands, as is shown in Figure 9.2, they gather along the bright fringes of the double-slit pattern, and few indeed alight upon the dark — with the very fringe-spacing the wave reckoning of topic 13 foretells, when λ = h/p is taken for the wavelength.
greekepic.23: No single particle makes a pattern alone. With both slits standing open, each particle bears itself as though it passed through both at once and interfered with its own self, and the pattern of interference dwells only in the reckoning, across the multitude of arrivals. Light and matter share this twofold nature — particle-like in their coming, wave-like in their scattering — and the single-particle double slit is the cleanest witness to the dual nature of them both.
greekepic.24: Key idea One particle, one dot; many particles, a wave pattern. Light and matter arrive as particles, yet are arrayed as waves — and this is the meaning of the words "dual nature."
greekepic.25: Connects Here ends the telling of the electron in this course: penned within circuits in Unit 1, then accelerated and turned aside by fields in Unit 3, it is named at last in Unit 4 neither pure particle nor pure wave. The same tale — an honoured model brought face to face with evidence it cannot answer — rises again in topic 17 as relativity.

cnpop.0: 电子是个不折不扣的粒子——有质量、有电荷、还能在屏幕上拉出一条看得见的轨迹。可你把这同一个电子朝晶体里一射，它居然像波一样发生了衍射。这一页我们要给物质也安排上属于它的波长，λ = h/p，然后顺着证据一路追到单粒子双缝实验。
cnpop.1: 到1924年，光的双重身份已经被实锤了：它能像波一样衍射、干涉（第13节），可它又偏偏以一份一份光子大小的能量传递能量、还携带动量（第14节）。路易·德布罗意一拍桌子：这种对称性凭啥只能单向走？如果一个波能像粒子一样带着动量跑，那一个运动的粒子也该有一个属于它的波长才对——这就是大名鼎鼎的德布罗意波长：
cnpop.2: 既然 p = mv，那物体越重、跑得越快，波长就越短。波动行为只有在波长跟它撞上的缝或障碍物差不多大的时候才看得见——就是第13节那个 λ/w 比值。就这一条门槛，直接解释了为啥物质波被人类憋了这么久才抓到。
cnpop.3: 来，一个质量 0.16 kg 的板球，以 40 m s⁻¹ 投出，动量 p = 0.16 × 40 = 6.4 kg m s⁻¹，于是 λ = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ ÷ 6.4 = 1.0 × 10⁻³⁴ m。这世上根本不存在这么窄的缝：这波长大约比一个质子的直径（约 1.7 × 10⁻¹⁵ m）还小 10¹⁹ 倍。原则上这球确实在衍射，可这衍射量小到任何仪器都别想测出来。
cnpop.4: 电子就完全是另一码事了。在 4.0 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹ 时，它的动量是 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 4.0 × 10⁶ = 3.6 × 10⁻²⁴ kg m s⁻¹，波长则是 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ ÷ (3.6 × 10⁻²⁴) ≈ 1.8 × 10⁻¹⁰ m——这跟晶体里原子面之间的间距是同一个数量级。也就是说，晶体恰好就是一个尺寸刚刚好的天然衍射结构。
cnpop.5: Trap 普朗克常数有两套单位，别搞混：6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s 和 4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵ eV s。在 λ = h/p 里动量用的是 kg m s⁻¹，这是个 SI 单位，所以这里永远只用 h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s。那个 eV s 的版本是配给用电子伏特表示的能量的，绝对绝对不要跟 kg m s⁻¹ 的动量搭在一起。
cnpop.6: Key idea 任何一个运动的物体都有一个德布罗意波长 λ = h/p。可波动行为只有在 λ 跟现成的开口差不多大时才看得见——而对日常物体来说，这事永远不会发生。
cnpop.7: 1927年，也就是德布罗意提出想法三年后，美国的戴维孙和革末、苏格兰的 G. P. 汤姆孙，分别拿电子束怼向晶体靶，结果——衍射出现了。在透射版本的实验里，一束电子穿过一层薄薄的多晶箔，荧光屏上就冒出一圈圈同心的亮环。那些规则排列、相隔几十分之一纳米的原子面，就充当了衍射结构。
cnpop.8: 亮环就是波的铁证。要是电子真的只是个纯粹的粒子、别的啥都不是，那这束电子打在屏上就该是中间一团亮、往边上慢慢淡掉。可实际上呢，明暗相间的环偏偏精准地出现在那些角度上——正是波被原子面散射后相互加强又相互抵消的地方。
cnpop.9: 最致命的一击是跟 X 射线对比，看图9.1。X 射线是电磁波，波长正好跟原子间距匹配，它们穿过同样的箔也能产生环状图样。当你把电子枪的加速电压调到让 h/p 等于 X 射线波长时，同波长的电子和 X 射线，会产生尺寸完全一样的环状图样——波长相等，物质衍射起来就跟光一模一样。要是两个图样尺度不一样，那就说明波长不同——λ/w 越大，环就铺得越开。
cnpop.10: 所以说，加速电压就直接决定了波长。从第3单元我们知道，一个电子从静止经过电位差 V 加速，会获得动能：
cnpop.11: Ishva 操作一根学校用的衍射管，里面的电子枪让电子从静止经过 500 V 的电位差加速，射进一层薄薄的石墨箔。她把每个电子都看成一个在均匀场里被加速的自由电荷。忽略相对论效应。
cnpop.12: Formula: 整条链子是 Ek = qV，接着 p = √(2mEk)，最后 λ = h/p。
cnpop.13: Substitution: Ek = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 500 = 8.00 × 10⁻¹⁷ J，于是 p = √(2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 8.00 × 10⁻¹⁷) = 1.207 × 10⁻²³ kg m s⁻¹。
cnpop.15: Answer: λ = 5.49 × 10⁻¹¹ m——大约是石墨里 2.13 × 10⁻¹⁰ m 原子面间距的四分之一，稳稳落在能衍射的范围里。回头验一下假设：v = p/m = 1.3 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹，这是 c 的 4.4%，所以忽略相对论效应是站得住脚的。这条链子还能反着走：测出一个波长，就能倒推动量、动能，进而锁定当初是多大的加速电压把它造出来的。
cnpop.16: Connects 这电子枪就是第3单元的老硬件：当时是 W = qV 定下了能量的尺度，而现在同样的加速电压通过 p 定下了一个波长。电子还是那个电子，新鲜的是这套波的描述。
cnpop.17: 把 λ = h/p 反过来读，它立马变成一句关于光的话：一个波长为 λ 的光子携带动量 p。公式表把这关系列了两遍——一遍当光子动量，一遍当德布罗意波长——就是因为它根本是同一条规则，光和物质通吃：
cnpop.18: 由此可知，一个光子和一个电子，只要波长相同，就带着相同的动量。可它们的能量不一样。光子的能量是：
cnpop.19: 在 λ = 2.0 × 10⁻¹⁰ m 时，光子和电子都携带 p = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ ÷ (2.0 × 10⁻¹⁰) = 3.3 × 10⁻²⁴ kg m s⁻¹。那个 X 射线光子的能量是 6.2 × 10³ eV，可电子的动能 Ek = p²/2m = 6.0 × 10⁻¹⁸ J = 38 eV——足足小了大约 165 倍。原因就俩字：速度。光子是以 c 把动量送出去的，所以 E = pc；而电子带着同样的动量，却只以 3.6 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹ 在跑，大约才 c 的八十分之一。
cnpop.20: Trap 波长对上了，对上的只是动量和衍射图样——能量永远对不上。一个电子和一个 X 射线光子，λ 相等就能拉出一模一样的环、带着一模一样的 p，可它们的能量却差了两个数量级。把 E = hf 往电子身上套，那是典型的用错公式：hf 只算光子能量，跟电子没关系。
cnpop.21: 衍射图样是由含有海量粒子的束流打出来的，这就留下了一个没解决的疑点：密集束流里的粒子说不定彼此互相影响，那这些条纹会不会只是个集体效应？现代的双缝实验把这个疑点摁死了。方法是把光源一直拧小，小到任何时刻仪器里只有一个光子——或者一个电子——然后把每一个的落点都记下来。
cnpop.22: 每个粒子都是整个儿到达的，落在单独一个点上：探测器上一个干干净净的小点，绝不会是整张图样的一个淡淡的副本。到达的方式是粒子式的。可这些点又不是均匀撒下来的。随着计数从几十涨到成千上万，就像图9.2展示的那样，它们扎堆聚在双缝图样的亮纹上，几乎没几个落在暗纹里——而且条纹间距，跟第13节那套波的分析用 λ = h/p 当波长算出来的，分毫不差。
cnpop.23: 单个粒子拼不出图样。当两条缝都开着时，每个粒子表现得就像它同时穿过了两条缝、还自己跟自己干涉，而那个干涉图样只在统计意义上存在，要跨越无数次到达才显形。光和物质共享这种双重身份——到达时像粒子，分布时像波——而单粒子双缝，就是这两者二象性最干净利落的证据。
cnpop.24: Key idea 一个粒子，一个点；许多粒子，一张波的图样。光和物质都是以粒子的身份到达、却以波的方式铺开——这就是“二象性”这仨字真正的意思。
cnpop.25: Connects 这是整门课程对电子的最后一次处理：第1单元里它还被关在电路里，第3单元里它被场加速、被场偏转，到了第4单元，它被描述成既不是纯粹的粒子、也不是纯粹的波。同样的剧情——一个站稳脚跟的模型，撞上了它解释不了的证据——会在第17节的相对论里再次上演。

ai.0: An electron is about as particle-like as things get — it has mass, it carries charge, and it leaves a track you can actually point to. Yet fire that very same electron through a crystal and it diffracts, like a wave spreading through a gap. This page hands matter its own wavelength, λ = h/p, and then follows the trail of evidence all the way to the single-particle double-slit experiment.
ai.1: By 1924 light had been caught playing both games at once: it diffracts and interferes like a wave (topic 13), yet it delivers energy in discrete photon-sized parcels and carries momentum (topic 14). Louis de Broglie asked the bold question — what if the symmetry runs both ways? If a wave can carry momentum like a particle, then surely a moving particle should have a wavelength of its own — the de Broglie wavelength:
ai.2: Since p = mv, the heavier and faster the object, the shorter its wavelength gets. And here’s the catch: wave behaviour only shows itself when the wavelength is roughly the size of the gap or obstacle the wave runs into — the λ/w ratio of topic 13. That one condition is the whole reason matter waves stayed hidden for so long.
ai.3: Take a cricket ball, mass 0.16 kg, bowled at 40 m s⁻¹. Its momentum is p = 0.16 × 40 = 6.4 kg m s⁻¹, so λ = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ ÷ 6.4 = 1.0 × 10⁻³⁴ m. No gap that narrow exists anywhere: the wavelength is about 10¹⁹ times smaller than the diameter of a proton (about 1.7 × 10⁻¹⁵ m). The ball does diffract, in principle — by an amount no instrument could ever hope to detect.
ai.4: An electron is a different story altogether. At 4.0 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹ its momentum comes to 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 4.0 × 10⁶ = 3.6 × 10⁻²⁴ kg m s⁻¹, and its wavelength is 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ ÷ (3.6 × 10⁻²⁴) ≈ 1.8 × 10⁻¹⁰ m — right on the same scale as the spacing between atomic planes in a crystal. Which means a crystal hands us a diffracting structure at exactly the right size.
ai.5: Trap Planck’s constant comes in two flavours: 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s and 4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵ eV s. In λ = h/p the momentum is in kg m s⁻¹, a good SI unit, so always reach for h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s. The eV s value belongs with energies written in electron-volts — never alongside momenta in kg m s⁻¹.
ai.6: Key idea Every moving object carries a de Broglie wavelength, λ = h/p. The trouble is that wave behaviour only surfaces when λ is comparable to the openings on offer — and for everyday objects, it simply never is.
ai.7: In 1927, just three years after de Broglie floated the idea, the evidence rolled in from two directions at once: Davisson and Germer in the United States and G. P. Thomson in Scotland aimed beams of electrons at crystalline targets and watched them diffract. In the transmission version, an electron beam passes straight through a thin polycrystalline foil and a set of concentric bright rings blooms across a fluorescent screen. The neatly spaced planes of atoms — a few tenths of a nanometre apart — play the part of the diffracting structure.
ai.8: Those rings are the giveaway — pure wave evidence. If electrons were particles and nothing more, the beam would simply slam into the screen as one central patch, fading out toward the edges. Instead, bright and dark rings alternate at precisely the angles where waves scattered by the atomic planes reinforce one another and cancel out.
ai.9: The clinching comparison is with X-rays, as shown in Figure 9.1. X-rays are electromagnetic waves with wavelengths that happen to match atomic spacings, and they fire off ring patterns through the same foils. Tune the electron gun’s accelerating voltage so that h/p equals the X-ray wavelength, and the punchline lands: electrons and X-rays of the same wavelength produce ring patterns with the same dimensions — at equal wavelength, matter diffracts exactly as light does. If two patterns come out at different scales, the wavelengths differ — the rings spread further as λ/w grows.
ai.10: So the accelerating voltage is what sets the wavelength. From Unit 3, an electron accelerated from rest through a potential difference V gains kinetic energy:
ai.11: Ishva is running a school diffraction tube, where an electron gun accelerates electrons from rest through a potential difference of 500 V and into a thin graphite foil. She treats each electron as a free charge accelerated through a uniform field. Ignore relativistic effects.
ai.12: Formula: the chain runs Ek = qV, then p = √(2mEk), then λ = h/p.
ai.13: Substitution: Ek = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 500 = 8.00 × 10⁻¹⁷ J, so p = √(2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 8.00 × 10⁻¹⁷) = 1.207 × 10⁻²³ kg m s⁻¹.
ai.15: Answer: λ = 5.49 × 10⁻¹¹ m — about a quarter of graphite’s 2.13 × 10⁻¹⁰ m atomic-plane spacing, comfortably inside the diffracting range. Now check the assumption: v = p/m = 1.3 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹, which is 4.4% of c, so leaving relativity out was perfectly fair. And the chain runs backwards just as well: a measured wavelength pins down the momentum, then the kinetic energy, and from there the very accelerating voltage that produced it.
ai.16: Connects The electron gun is Unit 3 hardware all over again: W = qV set the energy scale there, and now that same accelerating voltage sets a wavelength by way of p. The electrons haven’t changed at all — only the wave description is new.
ai.17: Now read λ = h/p the other way round and it turns into a statement about light: a photon of wavelength λ carries momentum p. The formula sheet lists the relation twice — once as photon momentum, once as the de Broglie wavelength — because it’s really one rule covering both light and matter:
ai.18: Which means a photon and an electron sharing the same wavelength must carry the same momentum. But their energies are another matter entirely. A photon’s energy is:
ai.19: At λ = 2.0 × 10⁻¹⁰ m, both a photon and an electron carry p = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ ÷ (2.0 × 10⁻¹⁰) = 3.3 × 10⁻²⁴ kg m s⁻¹. The X-ray photon’s energy is 6.2 × 10³ eV, but the electron’s kinetic energy is Ek = p²/2m = 6.0 × 10⁻¹⁸ J = 38 eV — roughly 165 times smaller. The reason comes down to speed: the photon delivers its momentum at c, so E = pc, while the electron lugs the same momentum along at just 3.6 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹, about an eightieth of c.
ai.20: Trap Matching wavelengths matches momenta and diffraction patterns — never energies. An electron and an X-ray photon of equal λ make identical rings and carry identical p, yet their energies part ways by two orders of magnitude. Slapping E = hf onto the electron is a wrong-formula error: hf is photon energy, and photon energy only.
ai.21: There’s a loose end, though. Diffraction patterns come from beams packed with enormous numbers of particles, which leaves one nagging objection: maybe the particles in a dense beam jostle and interact with each other, so the fringes are just some collective effect. The modern double-slit experiment closes that loophole for good. The source is dialled right down until only one photon — or one electron — is inside the apparatus at any moment, and the arrival point of each one is recorded.
ai.22: Each particle arrives whole, at a single point: one crisp dot on the detector, never a faint ghost of the full pattern. Arrival, in other words, is particle-like. And yet the dots refuse to land evenly. As the tally climbs from a few dozen to many thousands, as shown in Figure 9.2, they crowd along the bright fringes of the double-slit pattern and almost shun the dark ones — landing with exactly the fringe spacing the wave analysis of topic 13 predicts once λ = h/p is used as the wavelength.
ai.23: No single particle ever makes the pattern on its own. With both slits open, each particle behaves as if it slips through both at once and interferes with itself, and the interference pattern lives only in the statistics, built up across many arrivals. Light and matter both share this double life — particle-like in how they arrive, wave-like in how they spread — and the single-particle double slit is the cleanest evidence we have for the dual nature of the two.
ai.24: Key idea One particle, one dot; many particles, a wave pattern. Light and matter arrive as particles but distribute themselves as waves — and that, in a nutshell, is what “dual nature” means.
ai.25: Connects This is the last word on the electron in the course: penned into circuits in Unit 1, then accelerated and deflected by fields in Unit 3, and now, in Unit 4, revealed as neither a pure particle nor a pure wave. The same storyline — a trusted model running headlong into evidence it can’t explain — returns in topic 17 with relativity.
